attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# Architecture Notes
2	
3	## After cycle 1
4	- Change: tightened `runScenario` return type from `{ results: unknown[] }` to `{ results: { premium: number }[] }`
5	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — documents the shape tests exercise; `premium: number` field-name contract is now visible at the signature
6	- Naming established: `runScenario(scenario) -> { results: { premium: number }[] }`; field name `premium` confirmed (per example-mapping contract)
7	
8	## After cycle 2
9	- Change: extracted magic numbers in `runScenario` to module-level named constants `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE = 0.1` and `PROCESSING_FEE = 5`
10	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — these are global pricing-rule constants that more upcoming tests will reference; naming them centralises and documents intent at every use site. Rule 2 trumps APP (mass +5).
11	- Naming established: `FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE` (10% surcharge on first-insurance, applied to policy base), `PROCESSING_FEE` (flat 5 G added to every quote), `BASE_PREMIUM` (lookup table by item type); per-step local names `policyBase` (sum of item base premiums) and `firstInsurance` (surcharge amount, not boolean flag)
12	
13	## After cycle 3
14	- Change: extracted per-step pricing into a private module-level function `priceQuoteStep(step: QuoteStep) -> { premium: number }`; `runScenario` is now just `scenario.steps.map(priceQuoteStep)`
15	- Rationale: Rule 2 (reveals intent) — names the "price one quote step" concept that was previously anonymous inside `.map(...)`. Upcoming surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty, follow-up) have a clear home and won't bloat `runScenario`. Mass roughly neutral.
16	- Naming established: `priceQuoteStep(step)` is the canonical per-step pricing helper; future surcharges (curse, high-enchantment, loyalty discount, follow-up discount) should compose inside this function, not in `runScenario`
17	
18	## After cycle 4
19	- Change: extracted `itemBasePremium(item)` and `itemSurcharges(item)` helpers; named the `0.5` magic number as `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE`; renamed local `curseSurcharge` to `itemSurchargeTotal` (now an accumulator for all per-item surcharges, not just curse).
20	- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the `BASE_PREMIUM[item.type] ?? 0` lookup was duplicated across two reduces; now centralised in `itemBasePremium`. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `itemSurcharges(item)` is the natural extension point for upcoming high-enchantment (per-item) surcharge.
21	- Naming established: `itemBasePremium(item) -> number` (single source of base-premium lookup, will absorb rune-block logic later); `itemSurcharges(item) -> number` (sum of all per-item surcharges — add high-enchantment here next); `CURSE_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.5`; the local accumulator name `itemSurchargeTotal` (NOT `curseSurcharge`) reflects that it sums all per-item surcharges.
22	
23	## After cycle 5
24	- Change: rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return; added constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
25	- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
26	- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
27

content.text:
Now let me perform the refactoring analysis.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

Current names in `priceQuoteStep`:
- `policyBase` — sum of base premiums for items in this quote step ✓ reveals intent
- `itemSurchargeTotal` — sum of per-item surcharges (curse, high-enchantment) ✓ reveals intent
- `firstInsurance` — surcharge for first-insurance ✓ established
- `loyaltyDiscount` — discount for loyal customers ✓ clear
- `priceQuoteStep` — prices a single quote step ✓ clear
- `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS` ✓ clear

Function signature `priceQuoteStep(step, yearsWithMHPCO)` — passes a raw scalar `yearsWithMHPCO` from the customer object. This is a primitive-obsession-ish parameter list. As more customer-derived discounts/clauses come (follow-up discount, claim payouts), passing the whole customer would be cleaner. But for now, only loyalty depends on it, so this is reasonable. Keep.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass for `priceQuoteStep`

```
function priceQuoteStep(step, yearsWithMHPCO): { premium: number } {
  const policyBase = step.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemBasePremium(item), 0);
  const itemSurchargeTotal = step.items.reduce((sum, item) => sum + itemSurcharges(item), 0);
  const firstInsurance = policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
  const loyaltyDiscount = yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS
    ? policyBase * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;
  const premium = Math.ceil(policyBase + itemSurchargeTotal + firstInsurance - loyaltyDiscount + PROCESSING_FEE);
  return { premium };
}
```

Component count (rough):
- Constants: 2 (two `0` literals in reduces) = 2
- Bindings: ~14 (step, yearsWithMHPCO, policyBase, sum×2, item×2, itemSurchargeTotal, firstInsurance, FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE, loyaltyDiscount, LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS, LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE, premium, PROCESSING_FEE) ≈ 14
- Invocations: 7 (reduce×2, itemBasePremium, itemSurcharges, Math.ceil, + many but counting key calls) ≈ ~14 mass
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary) = 4
- Loops: 2 (two reduces) = 10
- Assignments: 0 = 0

Total ≈ 44

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

**Rule 1**: All 8 tests pass. ✓

**Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: The two reduces are conceptually doing the same thing on the same collection — summing a function over `step.items`. The intent of the function is: `total = sum of base + sum of surcharges + first-insurance + loyalty-adjustment + fee`. Two separate passes over `step.items` is fine, but a single pass that produces `{ base, surcharges }` per item would reveal "we compute two totals from the same items" more clearly.

**Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: There IS duplication — `step.items.reduce(...)` with `(sum, x) => sum + f(x)` is used twice. This is the well-known "sum-by" pattern duplicated. I can extract a helper `sumOver(items, f)` to remove the syntactic duplication.

**Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: A `sumOver` helper adds an element but removes a loop. Net mass should decrease since each reduce is mass ~12.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

Extract `sumOver` helper, replace the two reduces.

content.text:
All 8 tests still pass.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass for `priceQuoteStep`

```
const policyBase = sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium);
const itemSurchargeTotal = sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges);
const firstInsurance = policyBase * FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE;
const loyaltyDiscount = yearsWithMHPCO >= LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS
  ? policyBase * LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE : 0;
const premium = Math.ceil(policyBase + ... + PROCESSING_FEE);
```

Component count for `priceQuoteStep`:
- Constants: 1 (the `0` in loyaltyDiscount fallback) = 1
- Bindings: ~10 (step, yearsWithMHPCO, policyBase, itemSurchargeTotal, firstInsurance, FIRST_INSURANCE_RATE, loyaltyDiscount, LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS, LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE, premium, PROCESSING_FEE) ≈ 10
- Invocations: 4 (sumOver×2, Math.ceil, plus arithmetic +/-/* — counting major) ≈ ~8 mass
- Conditionals: 1 = 4
- Loops: 0 (loops are now inside `sumOver`) = 0

`priceQuoteStep` mass ≈ 23 (was ≈ 44, Δ -21 inside the function)

`sumOver` helper adds mass ≈ 10 (1 binding for xs, f, total, x; 1 invocation reduce + f; 1 loop = 5; 1 constant 0). Net global mass roughly neutral or slight reduction, but local clarity dramatically improved.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:
- Extracted generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper (Rule 3: no duplication)
- Replaced two `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, f)` calls
- `priceQuoteStep` body is now visibly "sum bases, sum surcharges, apply first-insurance, apply loyalty, add fee, ceil" with no reduce noise

Benefits:
- Removed duplicated reduce pattern (Rule 3)
- `priceQuoteStep` reveals intent more directly (Rule 2)
- Mass in the per-step pricer reduced from ~44 to ~23 (Δ -21); `sumOver` adds ~10 but is reused
- Future per-item aggregates (claim-payouts: sum of payouts over damages) have an obvious helper

## Step 8: Append to Architecture Notes

- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
input.new_string:
## After cycle 5
- Change: rewrote `itemSurcharges` from imperative `let surcharge = 0; if (…) surcharge += …` accumulator to two named expression bindings (`curseSurcharge`, `highEnchantmentSurcharge`) summed in the return; added constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3` and `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.
- Rationale: Rule 4 (fewest elements) — eliminated 3 assignments and a mutable `let`, reducing APP mass ~43 → ~26 (Δ -17). Rule 2 (reveals intent) — each surcharge component is now a named value at the call site, so future surcharges added inside `itemSurcharges` follow a clear pattern: `const xSurcharge = condition ? base * X_RATE : 0`.
- Naming established: pattern for new per-item surcharges is a named binding `<name>Surcharge = condition ? base * <NAME>_RATE : 0`; the return is a flat sum of these bindings (no mutable accumulator). Constants `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_SURCHARGE_RATE = 0.3`, `HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD = 5`.

## After cycle 6
- Change: introduced loyalty-discount logic in cycle 6's green phase (`LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`, applied to `policyBase` in `priceQuoteStep`); refactored by extracting a generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper and replacing the two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.
- Rationale: Rule 3 (no duplication) — the "sum f(x) over a collection" pattern was duplicated. Rule 2 (reveals intent) — `priceQuoteStep` now reads as a flat sequence of named pricing terms with no `reduce` noise; the per-step total mass dropped from ~44 to ~23. The helper will be reused for sum-of-payouts in upcoming claim tests.
- Naming established: `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` is the canonical "sum a function over a collection" helper — use it instead of inlining `xs.reduce((s, x) => s + f(x), 0)`. `LOYALTY_DISCOUNT_RATE = 0.2`, `LOYALTY_THRESHOLD_YEARS = 2`; loyalty discount is applied to `policyBase` only (NOT to surcharges, fee, or first-insurance), inside `priceQuoteStep`.

content.text: The reminder is appropriate — task #4 is the test that just completed its full Red/Green/Refactor cycle.

## Refactor Phase Complete

**Refactoring**: Extracted generic `sumOver<T>(xs, f)` helper; replaced two duplicated `step.items.reduce((sum, x) => sum + f(x), 0)` call sites in `priceQuoteStep` with `sumOver(step.items, itemBasePremium)` and `sumOver(step.items, itemSurcharges)`.

**Rules applied**:
- Rule 3 (no duplication): collapsed the duplicated "sum-by" reduce pattern
- Rule 2 (reveals intent): `priceQuoteStep` body now reads as a flat list of named pricing terms with no reduce noise

**Mass change** (priceQuoteStep local): ~44 → ~23 (Δ -21); `sumOver` adds ~10 but is reusable and will be used again for claim payouts.

**Tests**: 8 passing, 26 todo (all green).

**Naming evaluation**: All existing names (`policyBase`, `itemSurchargeTotal`, `firstInsurance`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `priceQuoteStep`, loyalty constants) still fit purpose — no renames needed.

Relevant files:
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-21_22-55-29_claim-office-lite-example-mapping_v4.2-shared-context_opus-4-7-portkey-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
